english: kaṅ-pa e' ˛p'rod ċe-na (khang pa e 'phrod ce na) it the question is, whether the house is likely to prosper.
Sch. = ˛p'ráb-pa ('phrab pa), འཕྲོལ་བ་ ˛p'ról-ba ('phrol ba) Sch. = ˛p'rál-ba ('phral ba).

v. ˛p'ro-ba ('phro ba); ˛p'ros ytóṅ-ba ('phros gtong ba) Schr., ˛p'ros-par byed-pa ('phros par byed pa) Sch. to spread, to pour forth, e.g. light, ˛p'ros ('phros) Tar. 48, 3, acc. to Schf.: a detailed work; but Tar. 143, 13?

1. the letter b, originally, and in the frontier districts still at the present day, corresponding to the English b; the pronunciation of it, however, varies a good deal in the different dialects of the country: in C. this letter, as an initial, is at present deep-toned and aspirated = bh; in Sp. as a final letter, it is softened down to w; and this softening of its sound prevails throughout Tibet in the substantive terminations ba (ba) and bo (bo), when preceded by a vowel or by ṅ (ng), r (r), l (l); as a prefix it is sounded in Bal. and Kh. = b or w. Regarding the irregularities in the pronunciation of initial db (db) v. the Phonetic Table. -- 2. num. figure: 15.

I. (also bá-mo (ba mo) Cs.?) cow, ˛dod-˛joi ba ('dod 'jo'i ba) v. ˛jo-ba ('jo ba); ba-kó (ba ko) cow-leather; ba-k'yú (ba khyu) herd of cows; ba-glaṅ (ba glang) v. below; ba-gċiṅ (ba gcing) urine of a cow; ba-lċi (ba lci) cow's dung; ba-ċu (ba chu), resp. -ċab (-chab) = ba-gċiṅ (ba gcing) (used by hindooizing Tibetans, the cow being sacred to the Hindoos); ba-nú (ba nu) 1. a cow's dug. 2. a stone resembling it in appearance Med.; ba-p'rúg (ba phrug) calf; ba-rmíg (ba rmig) a cow's hoofs; ba-rmíg-gi ċu (ba rmig gi chu) the water collected in the impression of a cow's foot on the ground, to denote a very small quantity of water Dzl.; ba-o (ba o) for bai ˛ó-ma (ba'i 'o ma); ba-rdzí (ba rdzi) cow-herd; ba-rá (ba ra) pen or stable for cows; ba-rú (ba ru) 1. a cow's horn. 2. vulg. cup for scarifying, the hollow tip of a cow's horn being used as such; ba-śá (ba sha) cow-beef. II. affix or so-called article, for pa (pa), to substantives the roots of which end with a vowel or with ṅ (ng), r (r), l (l), except when pa (pa) has its particular signification, as in ċu-pa (chu pa) etc. (v. pa (pa)); in adjectives it is either syn. with po (po) (as: dmár-bai mdaṅs (dmar ba'i mdangs), a ruddy complexion), or it denotes 'having' (= ... po-ċan (po can), as: sna-dmár-ba (sna dmar ba) or sna-dmár-po-ċan (sna dmar po can) having a red-dish trunk), or it is the sign of the verb formed from it (dmár-ba (dmar ba), to be red), or of the abstract substantive (dmár-ba (dmar ba), redness).

S.g. and elsewh.; Cs.: 'low wall, parapet'; acc. to my authorities a certain part of the timber work of a roof, something like pinnacle, battlement; so also Tar. 80, 21: the king with his retinue beheld the pinnacles of the Naga palace rising above the surface; v. nyúg-pa (nyug pa) 4.

ox, bull; *ba-laṅ tsogs* (ba lang tshogs) W., like an ox, stubborn, stupid; also dirty, filthy, nasty, for which our vulgar expression is swinish; ba-glaṅ-spyod (ba glang spyod) appellation for the western part of the globe, v. glíṅ (gling). -- 2. for bál-glaṅ (bal glang) Dzl.
Hind. बाटी, a large brass dish.
Stg., a small long-measure, 1/4 of a barley-corn.
W., also Sambh., 1. root. -- 2. stalk of fruit.
1. पताका, of which the word is a corruption acc. to Liś., an ensign
